did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a combination of deep learning features derived from a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and hand-crafted features (RSurf and Local Binary Patterns) for automated skin lesion classification (melanoma vs. benign lesions). The method employs SVM classifiers to process these features and achieve classification results on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel automated melanoma recognition system combining deep learning (CNN) and hand-crafted features (RSurf, LBP). It details a specific methodology, performs experimental evaluation on the ISIC dataset, and reports original classification results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) compared against other classifiers. The structure includes Introduction, Related Work, Proposed Method, Results, and Conclusion, which is characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Combining Deep Learning and Hand-Crafted Features for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated melanoma recognition system that combines deep learning features from a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with hand-crafted RSurf and Local Binary Pattern features. The method utilizes Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign lesions using dermoscopic images. The experimental evaluation was conducted on a large, publicly available dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), which included 900 training and 379 testing images. The proposed approach achieved a classification accuracy of 82.6%, with a sensitivity of 53.3% and specificity of 89.8%. When compared to other participants in the ISIC melanoma classification challenge, the method ranked as the fourth best performing classifier in terms of accuracy. The results indicate that combining hand-crafted and deep learning features is a promising approach for automated skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on automated recognition of melanoma. The dataset used for the experiment contained 173 images of melanoma in the training set and 75 images in the testing set.

The classification task distinguishes melanoma from benign lesions. The dataset contained 727 images of benign lesions in the training set and 304 images in the testing set. The paper notes that benign lesions are commonly known as moles.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant melanoma or benign lesion classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automated melanoma recognition by classifying dermoscopic images into two discrete categories: malignant melanoma and benign lesions. The method uses SVM classifiers to predict the class label for each image based on extracted features. Evaluation metrics include classification accuracy (82.6%), sensitivity (53.3%), and specificity (89.8%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive / ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large, publicly available dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the 2016 melanoma classification challenge. It contains dermoscopic images from various sources with different resolutions and image distortions.

use.training: Used for training the SVM classifiers with hand-crafted (RSurf, LBP) and deep learning (CNN) features.
use.testing: Used for independent testing and evaluation of the proposed method's classification accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
use.other: Used for comparison with state-of-the-art approaches and other participants in the ISIC melanoma classification challenge.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) on unseen data to avoid bias from cross-validation.
To benchmark the proposed method's performance against other participants in the ISIC melanoma classification challenge.

statistical_methods_used.method: Precision-Recall graph and ROC curve analysis
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and quantify classifier performance, specifically calculating the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC).
To summarize the precision-recall curve performance for each classifier.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, average precision, and area under the ROC curve, and compared against other classifiers in the ISIC challenge without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge D.split: Independent Testing Set (379 images: 304 benign, 75 melanoma)

First SVM Classifier (Hand-crafted Featu.description: SVM classifier using RSurf features and Local Binary Patterns (LBP) as input. Only one color channel (greyscale) was considered for texture-based feature extraction.

Second SVM Classifier (Deep Learning Fea.description: SVM classifier with Gaussian kernel and standardized predictors, using features derived from the second fully-connected layer of AlexNet (4096 elements).

Final Classifier (Combined Features).description: Combination of hand-crafted features and deep learning features. Scores (posterior probabilities) from both classifiers were used to determine the final classification label based on the highest absolute score.
Final Classifier (Combined Features).ranking: Fourth best performing classifier in terms of accuracy among participants in the ISIC melanoma classification challenge.

comparison_with_baselines.source: Top ten participants of the melanoma classification challenge [4]

ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge D.notes: The final classifier achieved an accuracy of 0.826, sensitivity of 0.533, and specificity of 0.898. The AUC was 0.780. The results are compared against state-of-the-art classifiers from the ISIC challenge. The paper notes that deep learning features confirmed their tendency to outperform hand-crafted features for this dataset, although the first classifier used only greyscale input while the second used RGB input for CNN feature extraction.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge 2012)
architecture_used.input_size: RGB input images (dimensions not explicitly specified, but standard AlexNet input is 227x227)

architecture_details.layers: Five convolutional layers (CONV), three fully-connected layers (FC), local response normalization (LRN) after first two CONV layers, max-pooling (MAX) layers, ReLU activation
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Features extracted from the second fully-connected layer (4096 elements/neurons)

training_details.hyperparameters: Not reported for CNN training (used pre-trained weights)

classification_or_output_head.input_to_head: 4096-dimensional feature vector from AlexNet's second FC layer
classification_or_output_head.alternative_head: SVM with hand-crafted features (RSurf and Local Binary Patterns) for comparison
classification_or_output_head.final_combination: Scores (posterior probabilities) from both SVM classifiers combined; final label determined by highest absolute score

preprocessing_techniques: Downsampling images so that the resolution in the longest axis does not exceed 800 pixels to speed up feature extraction.
Conversion of RGB images to greyscale by selecting the channel with the highest entropy for hand-crafted feature extraction (RSurf and LBP).
Application of a Gaussian filter with sigma (σ) = 0.5 prior to feature extraction to suppress noise, specifically for RSurf features.
conclusions: The proposed automated melanoma recognition system, which combines deep learning features from a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with hand-crafted RSurf and Local Binary Pattern features, achieved high classification accuracy (82.6%), sensitivity (53.3%), and specificity (89.8%) on the ISIC dataset.
The method ranked as the fourth best performing classifier in terms of accuracy among the top ten participants in the 2016 ISIC melanoma classification challenge.

Deep learning features confirmed their tendency to outperform hand-crafted features for this specific dataset, although the hand-crafted feature classifier used only greyscale input while the CNN classifier used RGB input.
Combining hand-crafted features with deep learning features provides a promising and high-performing solution for melanoma classification.

There is still room for improvement, and future work will focus on creating a new model specifically aimed at skin lesion images rather than using the pre-trained AlexNet model, which has limitations when applied to different research domains.
important_remarks: The study relies on a pre-trained AlexNet model (ImageNet) rather than a model specifically trained on skin lesion images, which the authors acknowledge has limitations when applied to different research domains.

The hand-crafted feature classifier (RSurf/LBP) utilized only a single greyscale channel (the one with highest entropy), whereas the deep learning classifier utilized full RGB input, creating an unfair comparison where the CNN had access to more color information.

The dataset contains images from various sources with different resolutions and image distortions/artifacts, which simulates real-world usage but introduces variability not controlled for in the analysis.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, and compared against challenge participants without significance testing.

The method achieved a sensitivity of 53.3%, indicating a high false-negative rate for melanoma detection, which is a critical clinical limitation despite the high specificity (89.8%) and accuracy (82.6%).
The results are based on a specific independent testing set from the 2016 ISIC challenge; generalizability to other datasets or clinical settings is not demonstrated.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed preprocessing beyond downsampling and Gaussian filtering, leaving potential biases in feature extraction unquantified.
